raw_case:
Facial nerve decompression of a PICA vessel loop: A case report


Introduction and importance

Facial nerve palsy is a common condition with various etiologies. However, compression due to a vessel loop is an exceptionally rare cause. This case report highlights the unusual presentation and management of facial nerve palsy caused by vascular compression, emphasizing the importance of considering rare etiologies in persistent cases.

Case presentation

We describe the case of a young female patient who presented with a history of right blepharospasm and facial muscle twitching for several years. Cranial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) vessel loop compressing the exit of the acoustofacial bundle at the level of the brainstem.

Clinical discussion

The patient's condition necessitated a microscopic surgical decompression to relieve the compression on the facial nerve. During the procedure, the facial nerve was freed and cushioned using Teflon. This intervention highlights the potential for surgical resolution in cases of facial nerve palsy caused by vascular compression, despite its rarity.

Conclusion

Postoperatively, the patient was free from blepharospasm and showed significant clinical improvement in facial muscle control. This case underscores the importance of considering vascular compression in the differential diagnosis of persistent facial nerve palsy and demonstrates the efficacy of surgical decompression in such cases.

Highlights

•Case of facial nerve compression treatment by surgical decompression•Video record of the surgical intervention and the result after the intervention1

Introduction

Facial nerve palsy is a medical disorder that is frequently observed and has multiple probable causes. It is characterized by the malfunctioning of the facial nerve. A variety of symptoms, such as weakening, twitching or paralysis of the muscles on one side of the face, drooping of the mouth or eyelid, and trouble closing the eye or grinning, can result from this neurological dysfunction [[1], [2], [3], [4]]. Healthcare practitioners must take into account less prevalent aetiologies in certain situations, even if viral infections, trauma, tumours, and Bell's palsy are among the more common causes of facial nerve palsy. One remarkable but atypical cause is compression brought on by a vascular loop [1,2].In this report, we present the case of a young female patient who had a PICA vessel loop compressing the facial nerve and leading to hemifacial twitching.2

Case presentation

The case involves a 32-year-old female patient who presented with a history of right blepharospasm and facial muscle twitching persisting for a few years. Previously, the condition was considered psychogenic and the patient was kept on carbamazepine. Physical exam revealed non-stop right facial twitching and weakness of House-Brackmann grade IV when the patient was instructed to execute facial muscle movements.A cranial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a unique finding: a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) vessel loop compressing the exit of the acoustofacial bundle at the level of the brain stem. Previously, the patient was seen by multiple neurologists who misdiagnosed the condition and failed to observe this finding on the MRI. The previous physicians have only read the report as stated by the patient.This intricate discovery highlighted the rare connection between the vascular anatomy and the neurological symptoms experienced by the patient. The PICA vessel loop, an aberration in the blood vessels supplying the brain, was identified as the culprit for the compression of the acoustofacial bundle at the site of exit from the brainstem. The acoustofacial bundle is a crucial structure that houses the facial nerve and plays a pivotal role in the control of facial muscles.In order to address this complex issue, the neurosurgical team opted for a microscopic surgical decompression procedure. The goal of the surgery was to meticulously free the facial nerve from the compression caused by the PICA vessel loop. Additionally, to provide a protective buffer and prevent further compression, a Teflon cushioning technique was employed during the surgical intervention.The use of microscopic surgical techniques allowed for precise and targeted decompression, minimizing the risk of damage to adjacent structures and optimizing the chances of a successful outcome. The Teflon cushioning served as a safeguard, ensuring that the facial nerve could function without hindrance once liberated from the vascular compression. The surgical details are illustrated in the video article and as below:1.Position: Park-bench position, head turned to left shoulder2.Incision: Retro-auricular3.Craniotomy: Retro-sigmoid craniotomy at J-point4.Dissection: Below J-point Dural incision, lower cranial nerve exposure and CSF drainage, retraction of cerebellum till exposure of the acousticofacial bundle5.Vessel Loop identification6.Vessel Loop dissectionPostoperatively, the patient experienced a remarkable improvement in her symptoms. The blepharospasm, which had been a persistent issue for several years, was completely alleviated. The patient was off carbamazepine. Furthermore, there was evident clinical improvement in the control of facial muscles, indicating the restoration of normal neurological function.3

Discussion

This case underscores the significance of a comprehensive diagnostic approach, combining advanced imaging modalities with meticulous surgical techniques. Diagnosis of this condition required the careful revision of the head MRI FIESTA (Fast Imaging Employing Steady-state Acquisition) sequences. The successful outcome not only relieved the patient from her distressing symptoms but also contributed valuable insights to the medical community regarding the association between vascular anomalies and facial nerve disorders. The utilization of microsurgical methods and Teflon exemplifies the evolving landscape of neurosurgical interventions aimed at providing tailored solutions for intricate neurological conditions.Facial nerve compression caused by a vascular loop is an extremely uncommon medical anomaly. This phenomenon is caused by a loop of blood vessels, usually veins or arteries, compressing the facial nerve. The vascular loop's pressure can obstruct the face nerve's normal function, which can result in facial nerve palsy's distinctive symptoms [[1], [2], [3]].To determine the precise site and kind of compression, a comprehensive clinical examination, imaging tests, and neurophysiological evaluations may be part of the diagnostic process in these circumstances. The fine intricacies of the facial nerve and the surrounding vasculature can be seen with the aid of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and angiography. Neurologists, neurosurgeons, and vascular experts may need to work together to manage facial nerve palsy brought on by vessel loop compression. In order to relieve the pressure on the facial nerve, treatment options may include surgical procedures to reduce the compression, such as microvascular decompression [4].The uncommon nature of vascular loop compression-induced facial nerve palsy highlights the significance of a thorough assessment when dealing with individuals who appear atypically. In order to better understand facial nerve problems and help healthcare practitioners diagnose and treat affected persons, more study and clinical awareness of these rare aetiologies are needed [3,4].4

Conclusion

This case study presents a patient with facial nerve compression caused by a PICA vessel loop, a rare condition often misdiagnosed. Through careful MRI analysis and a tailored surgical approach, the patient's symptoms were successfully alleviated. The case underscores the importance of a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and the effectiveness of advanced surgical techniques in managing complex neurological disorders. By raising awareness of this rare condition, healthcare providers can better diagnose and treat patients with similar symptoms.The following is the supplementary data related to this article.

Video 1

PICA vessel LOOP VIDEO anonymous.Video 1

Disclaimer

This care was reported according to the SCARE criteria [5].

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Ethical approval for this study (Ethical Committee at ZHUMC) was provided by the Ethical Committee of ZHUMC, Beirut, Lebanon on 12 May 2024.

Funding

No funding sources.

Author contribution

Ali Msheik: Study concept, design, data collection, writing the paper.Mohamad Yazbeck: Study concept, surgical intervention, design, data collection, writing the paper.

Guarantor

Ali Msheik is the guarantor.

Research registration number

n/a.

Declaration of competing interest

No conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 32-year-old female
- **Chief Complaint:** Persistent right blepharospasm and facial muscle twitching for several years
- **History of Present Illness:** Repeated, misdiagnosed presentations to neurologists; symptoms attributed to psychogenic causes; previously treated with carbamazepine without resolution.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported
- **Personal History:** None reported
- **Family History:** None reported
- **Physical Examination:** Non-stop right facial muscle twitching, facial muscle weakness with House-Brackmann grade IV on instructions to move facial muscles.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Cranial MRI revealed a PICA vessel loop compressing the exit of the acoustofacial bundle at the level of the brainstem.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - Common causes of facial nerve palsy (viral etiology, trauma, tumors, Bell's palsy) were initially considered but excluded based on the persistence of symptoms, imaging reports, and lack of response to conventional treatments.
   - The psychogenic etiology was misdiagnosed initially but ruled out after identifying an anatomical abnormality through MRI.

2. **Key Diagnostic Findings:**
   - The cranial MRI uncovered a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) loop compressing the acousticofacial bundle, a rare and specific anatomical cause of the symptoms.

3. **Rationale for Surgical Decompression:**
   - Compression by vascular structures identified as the direct cause of facial twitching and weakness mandated targeted surgical decompression for symptom resolution.

4. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - The thorough clinical analysis, sustained symptoms, and imaging focused specifically on vascular compression as the primary diagnosis rather than other neurological or systemic conditions.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Facial nerve compression by a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) vessel loop at the brainstem level.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Facial nerve compression by PICA vessel loop.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So, the patient is a 32-year-old female with persistent right blepharospasm and facial muscle twitching for several years. She's been to neurologists before, but they thought it was psychogenic and tried carbamazepine, which didn't work. Her physical exam shows continuous right facial twitching and weakness graded House-Brackmann IV when she tries to move her facial muscles. The MRI found a PICA vessel loop compressing the acoustofacial bundle at the brainstem.

First, I need to think about what causes facial muscle twitching and blepharospasm. Hemifacial spasm comes to mind. That's usually when a blood vessel compresses the facial nerve. Classic symptoms are involuntary twitching of muscles on one side of the face, starting maybe around the eye and spreading. The fact that she has a PICA loop compressing the facial nerve area on MRI points towards that. 

But wait, she also has facial weakness. Hemifacial spasm typically presents with twitching, but weakness isn't a common feature unless there's some overlap with another condition or maybe the compression is causing both irritation (leading to spasms) and some degree of nerve damage (leading to weakness). House-Brackmann IV is moderate to severe weakness, which makes me think there's more than just irritation. 

Blepharospasm is another condition, but that's usually bilateral and not associated with facial weakness. Since her symptoms are unilateral, that's more in line with hemifacial spasm. The fact that she didn't respond to carbamazepine, which is sometimes used for neuropathic pain or trigeminal neuralgia, suggests that maybe the underlying cause isn't just a nerve irritation that can be managed with meds. 

Her MRI showing vascular compression at the brainstem exit of the facial nerve is a big clue. The PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar artery) loop compressing the nerve is a known cause of hemifacial spasm. The facial nerve exits the brainstem there, and vascular compression can lead to ectopic nerve impulses causing the spasms. 

Other differentials might include facial myokymia, which is more of a rippling muscle activity, but usually associated with MS or other brainstem lesions. But the MRI here shows a vascular compression, not a demyelinating lesion. Psychogenic causes were considered before, but the MRI finding and lack of response to previous treatment make that less likely. 

Putting it all together: the unilateral facial twitching, the MRI evidence of vascular compression, the lack of response to medication, and the facial weakness (which could be due to chronic compression leading to nerve damage) all point towards hemifacial spasm caused by neurovascular compression from the PICA loop. The House-Brackmann grade IV weakness might be a result of long-standing compression affecting the nerve's function beyond just causing spasms. 

So the final diagnosis should be hemifacial spasm secondary to vascular compression by the PICA. That's the most fitting based on the presented case.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with chronic unilateral right facial muscle twitching (blepharospasm and facial myoclonus) and facial weakness (House-Brackmann IV), suggesting a focal cranial nerve dysfunction. Hemifacial spasm (HFS) is a key consideration, as it classically involves involuntary contractions of muscles innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII).

<step 2> The lack of response to carbamazepine, often used for neuropathic pain or trigeminal neuralgia, argues against purely irritative or inflammatory etiologies. Psychogenic attribution in prior evaluations is inconsistent with the objective MRI finding of a PICA vessel loop compressing the acoustofacial bundle (CN VII and VIII exit zone).

<step 3> The cranial MRI finding of neurovascular compression at the brainstem (root exit zone of CN VII) is pathognomonic for hemifacial spasm. Vascular loops (e.g., PICA, AICA) mechanically irritating the facial nerve cause ectopic excitation, leading to spasms and, in chronic cases, secondary weakness due to nerve damage.

<step 4> Alternative diagnoses like benign fasciculation syndrome, focal seizures, or tardive dyskinesia are less likely given the unilateral, persistent nature and imaging evidence. Facial myokymia (e.g., from brainstem lesions) is excluded by the absence of MRI findings other than vascular compression.

### Answer:
Hemifacial spasm due to neurovascular compression by the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) loop.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with chronic unilateral right facial muscle twitching (blepharospasm and facial myoclonus) and facial weakness (House-Brackmann IV), suggesting a focal cranial nerve dysfunction. Hemifacial spasm (HFS) is a key consideration, as it classically involves involuntary contractions of muscles innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII).

The lack of response to carbamazepine, often used for neuropathic pain or trigeminal neuralgia, argues against purely irritative or inflammatory etiologies. Psychogenic attribution in prior evaluations is inconsistent with the objective MRI finding of a PICA vessel loop compressing the acoustofacial bundle (CN VII and VIII exit zone).

The cranial MRI finding of neurovascular compression at the brainstem (root exit zone of CN VII) is pathognomonic for hemifacial spasm. Vascular loops (e.g., PICA, AICA) mechanically irritating the facial nerve cause ectopic excitation, leading to spasms and, in chronic cases, secondary weakness due to nerve damage.

Alternative diagnoses like benign fasciculation syndrome, focal seizures, or tardive dyskinesia are less likely given the unilateral, persistent nature and imaging evidence. Facial myokymia (e.g., from brainstem lesions) is excluded by the absence of MRI findings other than vascular compression.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial considerations were made for common causes like viral etiology, trauma, tumors, and Bell's palsy, which were excluded due to persistent symptoms, imaging results, and lack of response to conventional treatments.
The psychogenic etiology was initially suspected but ruled out upon identification of an anatomical abnormality via MRI.
Cranial MRI revealed a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) loop compressing the acousticofacial bundle, a rare anatomical cause of the symptoms.
Compression by vascular structures was identified as the direct cause of facial twitching and weakness, necessitating targeted surgical decompression for symptom relief.
A thorough clinical analysis, sustained symptoms, and imaging focused exclusively on vascular compression as the primary diagnosis, excluding other neurological or systemic conditions.
Final diagnosis: Facial nerve compression by a posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) vessel loop at the brainstem level.
